questions.question: Rust's ownership rules: at any given time, a value can be borrowed by:
options: Any number of shared `&T` references OR exactly one exclusive `&mut T` — never both
questions.explanation: Exclusive XOR shared. This is the key rule that eliminates data races and most use-after-free bugs at compile time. Internally, the rule is called 'aliasing XOR mutation' — analogously to the Rust standard library's `Cell`/`Mutex` types that re-introduce shared mutation safely via runtime checks.

questions.question: Why does `let s2 = s1;` move ownership when `s1: String`, but copy when `s1: i32`?
options: `String` owns heap memory; allowing two owners would risk double-free. `i32` is a primitive bit pattern with no resources; copying is free.
questions.explanation: Types that own external resources (heap memory, file handles, sockets) MUST NOT be silently duplicated — that would double-free on drop. They move. Primitive types (i32, bool, fixed arrays of these) implement the `Copy` trait — a marker telling the compiler 'bitwise copy is safe.'

options: `first` is a shared borrow. `v.push(4)` takes a mutable borrow — and may reallocate, invalidating `first`. The borrow checker rejects shared + mutable at once.
questions.explanation: The classic 'iterator invalidation' bug from C++ — caught at compile time. `&v[0]` would dangle after a reallocating push. Rust's borrow checker sees this statically. Fix: copy `*first` to a local before pushing, or shrink the scope of `first`.

questions.question: When does Rust call a value's `Drop::drop` method?
options: Automatically, when the owning binding goes out of scope (matches RAII). On panic-unwind too, unless the panic aborts.
questions.explanation: Drop fires deterministically at scope exit (or earlier with `mem::drop`). No GC, no finalizers. This makes resource cleanup predictable: a `MutexGuard`'s drop releases the lock; a `File`'s drop closes the FD; a `Vec<T>`'s drop frees the buffer. The compiler inserts the drop calls; the user never does.

questions.question: What's the difference between `&T` and `&mut T`?
options: `&T` is shared (many can coexist; read-only). `&mut T` is exclusive (only one at a time; can mutate). The borrow checker enforces this XOR property.
Only `&mut T` is real; `&T` is for documentation
questions.explanation: `&T`: 'I want to read it; others can read too.' `&mut T`: 'I want sole access; others can't even read while I have this.' This rule lets the compiler safely reorder/optimize through borrows, and it directly prevents data races (Send/Sync extensions in Phase 13).

questions.question: When should you call `.clone()` in Rust?
options: When you genuinely need a deep copy AND can afford it. Often the right move is to take a `&T` borrow instead — clone defeats Rust's zero-cost abstraction.
questions.explanation: Clone is an explicit, possibly-expensive duplication. Reaching for it is often a sign the code structure is wrong — try `&T` borrows or `Rc<T>`/`Arc<T>` shared ownership first. But sometimes (returning owned data, cross-thread move) clone is exactly right.

_meta.title: Ownership And Borrowing The Rust Model
